label: Intro
title: The Beginning
content:
Hello. I am Sumo — the villain of this story.

Here, I am going to write about every moment I spent with her. We will call her "Sumi." I know that's not a name, but I used to call her that — Sumo ki Sumi. This is not a love story. It's not a breakup story either. This is my story — a story about how I lost something precious that was always mine, because of my own wrong steps. She never wanted to leave, and neither did I. But I made things so difficult, so unbearable, that today she doesn't even want to see my face.

I am married now. But I still miss her.

I have kept all her memories with me — every gift she ever gave, every picture we took together. I keep them at a friend's place, where I go sometimes just to sit with them. To look at her face in those photographs. I never moved on. She did, I'm sure.

She lives near my house. I cross by her street almost every day — not because it's on the way, but because I hope. Maybe today I'll catch a glimpse of her on the balcony, or standing outside. I would pay millions just to see her for a second. In my eyes, she was the most beautiful girl in the world. But like most people, she had her insecurities — I'm not that beautiful, she'd say. And I always had only one answer:

"Everyone in the world is beautiful. You just need the right eyes to see it. And I am your eyes."

I live in Jaipur, Rajasthan. I am a motion and graphic designer by profession. I am a boring guy — but don't worry, this book is not about me. It's all about her. This is my first book, and my last. I just don't want to forget the only memories where I was truly happy.

So. Let's begin.

label: Chapter 1
title: The First Meet
content:
I was around 12 or 13 years old when I first met her.

We had actually been in the same school since 4th class, but our sections were always separate — she was in Section A, I was in Section B. We passed through the same corridors for years without ever really meeting. Then came Class 6, and the school decided to combine our sections. That's when I first saw her.

She used to hate me from the moment we met. And honestly? I didn't care. Not until Class 10th anyway.

So let's fast forward.

Class 11th. A new batch, a new beginning, and summer holidays had just ended. It was an ordinary day — the kind of day where nothing is supposed to happen. Then our school director walked into class and told everyone to get out. She rearranged the entire seating. And that rearrangement changed everything.

But before I tell you about that — let me tell you about the walk.

Her home was maybe 500 meters from school. Mine was 3 km away. She always walked home. I never did, because my dad used to pick me up every day. But one afternoon, my dad didn't come.

So I decided to walk.

I was nervous. Quiet streets feel longer when you're alone. Then I spotted Sumi ahead of me, walking home by herself. I picked up my pace. Matched her speed. Reached her side.

I said — "Hey."

I still remember her face. She looked at me like I had personally offended her existence. No reply. Not a word. We walked nearly 300 meters side by side — me trying everything I could think of to start a conversation, her acting like I was completely invisible. Then her house arrived, she turned in, and I kept walking alone toward my 3 km home.

That was not our beginning. But it was the first hint that she was going to be difficult — and somehow that made it more interesting.

Now, back to the seating arrangement.

The director pointed at the three of us and said — Sumi, Sumo, and Sunil will sit together.

Let me explain the logic. I was the most academically challenged student in the class. She was the most intelligent girl. Sunil was somewhere near my level. The teacher's theory was simple — if we sat together, maybe some of her brilliance would rub off on us.

Sweet theory.

What actually happened was the opposite. Instead of her making us better students, we made her a bad student. Majority wins, after all.

But that seat — that one chair next to hers — was the day I finally got to talk to Sumi for real. For the first time.

And nothing was the same after that.
